front: What is the purpose of the `alarm` function in Unix system programming?
back: The `alarm` function is used to set a timer that will send the `SIGALRM` signal to the calling process after a specified number of real (wall-clock) seconds.

The `pause` function suspends the calling process until any signal is caught. Upon catching a signal and the signal handler returns, `pause` also returns with a value of -1 and `errno` set to `EINTR`.

front: How can you create a simple sleep function using `alarm` and `pause`?
back: A simple (but flawed) sleep function can be implemented by setting an `alarm` for the desired seconds and then calling `pause` to wait for the `SIGALRM` signal.

front: What is a major caveat when using the `alarm` function?
back: Each process can have only one active alarm timer. A new call to `alarm` will cancel any previously set alarm that has not yet expired and returns the number of seconds remaining on the previous timer.

front: What signal is generated by the `alarm` function when the timer expires?
back: The `alarm` function generates the `SIGALRM` signal (signal number 14) when the specified time interval elapses.
